verdict_reason: BG 2.21 is the rhetorical climax of the imperishable doctrine — 'how can he kill or be killed?' is not light material. The engineering layer treats the four properties (indestructible / eternal / unborn / undecaying) with the seriousness they deserve, mapping each to a distinct contract-level property rather than collapsing them into 'change is fine.' The 'how can he kill or be killed?' rhetorical move is preserved as the agent-over-predecessors-code / killed-by-deprecation doublet. The counter-example explicitly disqualifies any flippant read (rewrites that drop unspecified behaviours do kill something real). The Rails→Go scenario takes the Rails author's 18,000 lines seriously enough to name the design decisions encoded there. A Sanskrit scholar would feel the verse has been honoured by being mapped to a structurally analogous engineering decision-architecture, not trivialised into 'don't be sad about deleted code.' PASS.
